entities.name: śabda-śaktayaḥ
entities.cite: the powers of words — the whole system of them, from mantra-efficacy (1.138) to world-binding (1.118)

entities.name: svabhāva-jña
entities.cite: those who know the nature of things

axioms.name: avasthita
axioms.cite: the param padam is unmoved through the entire cycle of speech's emanation and return

axioms.name: drsyante
axioms.cite: drsyante svabhava-jnaih bhavanam: are *seen* by knowers of the natures of things

axioms.name: saksad_drsya
axioms.cite: the deep powers of speech are not inferred from usage but beheld by those whose cognition has climbed the very gradient 1.142 mapped

denials.name: anumita
denials.cite: the deep powers of speech are not inferred from usage

claims.name: drsyante
claims.cite: the powers of words are seen by those who know the nature of things

rejected_readings.label: real_transformation
rejected_readings.rendering: As the supreme word-station manifests through utterance and returns to unity, it undergoes real change in substance.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts parinamate (real transformation of substance), which the axiom and commentary explicitly deny. The param padam stands unaltered; the commentary invokes vivarta (appearance without loss of unity) precisely to rule out parinama. The hierarchy doctrine of 1.75-77 and 1.101 is lifted to the summit to demonstrate invariance through the entire emanation-return cycle.

rejected_readings.label: inferential_access
rejected_readings.rendering: The powers of words are discovered by scholars through careful analysis of linguistic patterns and inference.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly denies this: powers are directly seen/beheld (drsyante), not inferred from usage. This is vision-knowledge (rsi's mode), not inference. The rendering mistakes the epistemology: powers are not derived conclusions but immediate vision, visible only to those whose cognition has reached the level where they operate.

notes: The verse pairs ontological invariance (param padam unmoved through emanation-return) with epistemological immediacy (word-powers directly visible to the knower of natures). The three entities suffice: param padam as the absolute summit, sabda-saktayah as the operative powers, svabhava-jna as the cognition that beholds them. No strain in the six sorts. Contested: the referent of 'tat' and whether kriyamanam describes the param padam's emanation or smrti-work (analysis-synthesis)—the contract follows the commentary's dominant reading of param padam as the fixed summit persisting through all levels.
